summary: American novelist and poet, a central figure of the Beat Generation. Best known for On the Road, typed on a single 120-foot scroll, about Sal Paradise and Dean Moriarty's cross-country travels. Also wrote The Dharma Bums, Mexico City Blues, Desolation Angels, and other works exploring Buddhism and 'spontaneous prose.'

works.name: On the Road
works.description: Kerouac's best-known novel, typed on a single 120-foot-long scroll of paper in a Benzedrine-fueled session of 'spontaneous prose' — prompting Truman Capote's quip: 'That's not writing; that's typing.' The novel follows the writer Sal Paradise and the 'holy goof' Dean Moriarty as they drive to Denver and meet the poet Carlo Marx (modeled on Allen Ginsberg). Sal meets 'the cutest little Mexican girl' while riding a bus and picks cotton with Terry and Ricky in Sabinal before going to Paterson for Christmas. Another character puts himself in an 'orgone' box to increase his sexual energy.

works.name: The Dharma Bums
works.description: A novel that fictionalizes Gary Snyder as Japhy Ryder and Kerouac as Ray Smith. The narrator climbs Matterhorn Peak with Japhy. The novel ends with Kerouac staffing a fire lookout on Desolation Peak. The book reflects Kerouac's (sometimes muddled) engagement with Buddhism and was inspired by the Beat scene including the Six Gallery reading of Allen Ginsberg's 'Howl.'

clues.clue: Fictionalizes Six Gallery reading of Ginsberg's 'Howl'
examples: Kerouac's muddled Buddhism also inspired his novel The Dharma Bums, which fictionalizes the Six Gallery reading

works.name: Mexico City Blues
works.description: A long poem in 242 'choruses' with Buddhist influences, written in Mexico City. Reflects Kerouac's jazz-influenced approach to poetry and his engagement with Buddhist themes.

clues.clue: Poem in 242 'choruses' with Buddhist influences, written in Mexico City
examples: whose 'Blues' title a Kerouac poem in 242 'choruses' with Buddhist influences
This poet's warped perception of Buddhism is displayed in the long poem Mexico City Blues

works.name: Spontaneous Prose and Essays
works.description: Kerouac articulated his literary technique in 'Essentials of Spontaneous Prose,' which lists forgoing periods for the 'vigorous space dash' to imitate a 'jazz musician drawing breath between outblown phrases.' He equated writing with jazz musicians' technique and described an injunction to write 'in accordance with laws of orgasm.' He also used his own blood to write parts of the novella I Bid You Love Me as part of a Nietzschean quest for 'Self-Ultimacy.' He wrote many simple three-line American haikus, often eschewing the seventeen-syllable requirement. Norman Mailer's description of jazz as 'the music of orgasm' prefigures Kerouac's ideas.

clues.clue: 'Essentials of Spontaneous Prose': forgoing periods for 'vigorous space dash' to imitate jazz musician drawing breath

clues.clue: Wrote American haikus eschewing seventeen-syllable requirement

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Kerouac wrote Desolation Angels, which opens at the fire lookout on Desolation Peak. The Scripture of the Golden Eternity is a prose-poem describing 'innumerable holy ghosts, buddhies, and savior gods... smiling.' Vanity of Duluoz fictionalizes Lucien Carr as Claude de Maubris. The Town and the City replaces Carr with Kenneth Wood; the protagonist debates fascism with Nick Meade after leaving his Columbia professorship to join the Merchant Marines. And the Hippos Were Boiled in Their Tanks was co-written with William S. Burroughs about David Kammerer's killing by Lucien Carr. The Sea is My Brother is another early novel. Lawrence Ferlinghetti wrote a 'Canticle of Jack Kerouac' set in Lowell, Massachusetts. Ti-Jean is a recurring name for Kerouac.

clues.clue: Desolation Angels opens at fire lookout on Desolation Peak
examples: A Kerouac novel titled Desolation Angels opens at that look-out

comprehensive_summary:
Jack Kerouac is an American novelist and poet, a central figure of the Beat Generation. His best-known novel, On the Road, was typed on a single 120-foot-long scroll of paper during a Benzedrine-fueled burst of 'spontaneous prose,' prompting Truman Capote's quip 'That's not writing; that's typing.' The novel follows the writer Sal Paradise and the 'holy goof' Dean Moriarty as they drive to Denver and meet the poet Carlo Marx, modeled on Allen Ginsberg. Sal meets 'the cutest little Mexican girl' on a bus and picks cotton with Terry and Ricky in Sabinal before going to Paterson for Christmas.

The Dharma Bums fictionalizes Gary Snyder as Japhy Ryder, with Kerouac as Ray Smith climbing Matterhorn Peak. The novel reflects Kerouac's muddled Buddhism and ends with him staffing a fire lookout on Desolation Peak. It fictionalizes the Six Gallery reading where Allen Ginsberg debuted 'Howl.'

Mexico City Blues is a long poem in 242 'choruses' with Buddhist influences. Kerouac articulated his technique in 'Essentials of Spontaneous Prose,' which advocates forgoing periods for the 'vigorous space dash' to imitate a 'jazz musician drawing breath between outblown phrases' and writing 'in accordance with laws of orgasm.' He wrote American haikus, eschewing the seventeen-syllable requirement, and used his own blood to write parts of I Bid You Love Me.

Desolation Angels opens at the fire lookout on Desolation Peak. The Scripture of the Golden Eternity describes 'innumerable holy ghosts, buddhies.' He co-wrote And the Hippos Were Boiled in Their Tanks with William S. Burroughs about Lucien Carr's killing of David Kammerer. Vanity of Duluoz fictionalizes Carr as Claude de Maubris. Lawrence Ferlinghetti wrote a 'Canticle of Jack Kerouac' set in Lowell, Massachusetts, and Kerouac is repeatedly analogized to Ti-Jean.
